sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient, a 54-year-old male, presents with a history of stabbing, shooting pain in the right cheekbone area of his face, which has been ongoing for approximately 2-3 months.
The pain is exacerbated by temperature changes, cold wind, and is often accompanied by numbness on the right side of his face.
He reports that the pain is excruciating, often lasting for a few minutes to an hour, and is described as a 10 out of 10 in severity.
He denies any sensory loss, weakness, or numbness in other areas of his face, including the left side.
He reports that his vision and smile appear unchanged, and he has not experienced any weakness on that side of his face.
He has tried ibuprofen, Motrin, and Tiger Balm, which have not provided relief.
He has a history of occasional headaches, but no history of migraines.
He has not had any previous conditions like multiple sclerosis or brain tumors.
Past medical history: The patient has a history of occasional headaches, but no history of migraines, multiple sclerosis, or brain tumors.
Review of systems: The patient reports numbness on the right side of his face, but denies any other symptoms.
Current medications: Tylenol (dosage not specified) for occasional headaches.
Objective:
Vital signs: Not mentioned.
Physical exam: The patient was able to follow the doctor's finger in both directions and showed symmetrical teeth.
He reported feeling a little bit numb on the right side of his face when the doctor rubbed his fingers next to his ear.
The doctor poked the patient's face with a needle and reported that the patient felt a dull sensation on the right side and a sharp sensation on the left side.
Test Results: MRI results show a small blood vessel abutting and perhaps pinching the trigeminal nerve.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has symptoms consistent with trigeminal neuralgia, a condition where nerve compression causes shooting electrical pain in the face.
Problem list: Trigeminal neuralgia; confirmed.
Plan:
I prescribed Tegretol for the patient to start, as it is a first-line treatment for trigeminal neuralgia.
The patient will need to undergo genetic testing to ensure the medication is safe for him.
If the pain continues, we will discuss surgical options to treat the pain.
Follow-up: The patient will return in one month to check the effectiveness of the medication and to discuss further treatment options if necessary.
